title: Risotto nero
subtitle: Squid ink risotto with white wine and a butter finish
excerpt: Risotto nero is one of the most striking dishes you can make: the rice turns completely black from the squid ink, which also adds a clean, briny depth to the flavour. The technique is the same as any risotto, with the ink added towards the end.
sourceNotes: Venetian dish from the Veneto region of northern Italy. Risotto al nero di seppia (squid ink risotto) is a classic of Venetian cooking, traditionally made with cuttlefish rather than squid. The dish is found along the Adriatic coast and is now made across Italy and internationally.

content.text: Risotto nero requires the same patience as any risotto: the stock must be added gradually, and the rice must never be left to dry out. The squid ink goes in after the wine has been absorbed, giving the rice time to cook through before the ink colours everything black. The final butter and cheese finish is essential; without it the risotto has no richness.

items.prepNote: divided: 20 g for cooking, 40 g for finishing

content.text: Keep the fish stock hot in a separate saucepan over a low heat. Heat the olive oil and 20 g of butter in a large saucepan over a medium heat. Add the onion and cook for 8 minutes until soft. Add the garlic and cook for 1 minute.
Add the rice and stir for 2 minutes until the grains look slightly translucent at the edges. Pour in the white wine and stir until fully absorbed.

Add the squid ink to the rice and stir to coat all the grains. Begin adding the warm stock one ladleful at a time, stirring after each addition and waiting until each ladle is mostly absorbed before adding the next. Continue for 18 to 20 minutes until the rice is just tender with a slight bite.

Remove from the heat. Beat in the remaining 40 g of cold butter and the parmesan vigorously. Cover and rest for 2 minutes. The risotto should flow slowly when the pan is tilted. Season with salt. Scatter with parsley if using and serve immediately.

items.symptom: The risotto is too thick and stodgy
items.cause: Overcooked, or not enough stock added.
items.fix: Remove from heat while the rice still has a slight bite. The risotto will continue to thicken as it rests. Add a final splash of warm stock if it tightens too much.

items.symptom: The squid ink is very faint
items.cause: Only one sachet was used, or the ink was added too early and lost colour.
items.fix: Two 4 g sachets gives a deep black. Add the ink before the stock so it has time to colour the grains.

items.symptom: The rice is chalky in the middle
items.cause: Not enough cooking time, or stock added too quickly.
items.fix: Add each ladleful gradually and taste as you go. 18 to 20 minutes is a guide, not a guarantee.

content.text: Risotto nero comes from Venice, where cuttlefish and squid have been part of the diet for centuries. The ink was first extracted from fresh cuttlefish and added to the pot; today it is sold in sachets. The dish is one of the most striking in Italian cooking. A completely black plate is entirely correct.
